questions.question: Why can accepting all incoming work during overload be worse than rejecting some of it quickly?
options: Because growing queues and in-flight work can destroy latency for every request, including critical ones.
Because overload only matters when disk is full.
Because fast rejections automatically make every client back off correctly.
Because accepting work prevents autoscaling from ever happening.
questions.explanation: Unbounded acceptance often turns a partial overload into a full-service collapse.

questions.question: What is admission control fundamentally deciding?
options: Which work the system is still willing to accept given current capacity and priorities.
Which logs should be sampled during normal operation.
How many replicas every database table should have.
Whether retries are ever allowed in asynchronous systems.
questions.explanation: Admission control is about selective acceptance under constrained capacity.

questions.question: Which signal is often stronger than raw queue length for overload decisions?
options: Queue age, because it shows whether waiting work is already becoming stale or harmful.
The number of markdown headings in the runbook.
Average code review turnaround time.
Total process uptime in days.
questions.explanation: Old queued work is often a clearer sign of overload than count alone.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of reserving capacity for high-priority traffic?
options: Critical flows stay healthy under stress, but average utilization can be lower in quiet periods.
All best-effort work becomes free to process later.
Clients stop needing retry logic entirely.
The service no longer needs any downstream health signals.
questions.explanation: Reserved capacity improves reliability for important work at some efficiency cost.

questions.question: Which failure is most likely if the service queues work even when the downstream database is saturated?
options: The service looks alive briefly, but queue age and end-to-end latency explode while the root bottleneck remains unsolved.
The database automatically gains more write throughput.
The queue becomes a reliable cache for all repeated requests.
Critical traffic stops needing classification.
questions.explanation: Queueing cannot fix a hard downstream bottleneck; it often just delays visible pain.

questions.question: What metric best checks whether admission control is protecting the right traffic?
options: Success rate and latency of critical traffic during overload windows.
Total number of service owners in the org chart.
The amount of unused RAM on developer laptops.
The count of dashboard panels per team.
questions.explanation: The goal is not only overall throughput, but preservation of the most important flows.

questions.question: Why should overload responses consider client retry behavior?
options: Because a shed request can trigger a retry storm unless callers have clear backoff guidance and budgets.
Because retries automatically guarantee fairness across tenants.
Because clients can never distinguish overload from success.
Because server-side limits stop mattering once a request is rejected.
questions.explanation: Load shedding is safer when the caller behavior it triggers has also been considered.

questions.question: What weakens an interview answer about overload most?
options: Treating autoscaling as the only strategy and ignoring queue limits, priorities, and fast rejection.
Explaining that some work may need to be dropped to save the core path.
Discussing both local and downstream bottlenecks.
Calling out fairness trade-offs during spikes.
questions.explanation: Senior-level overload answers protect the system before scaling catches up, not after.
